questions.scenario:
An operator edits `jenkins.yaml` to add a new credential and update the system message. They want the change live without a controller restart.

What is the right action?

options.text: Trigger a CasC reload from Manage Jenkins > Configuration as Code > Reload existing configuration.
Restart the controller because every YAML edit requires a JVM restart.
Edit the same values through the UI and leave the YAML stale.
Write directly to `$JENKINS_HOME/config.xml`.

questions.explanation: The article says a CasC reload re-reads the YAML and applies most day-to-day config changes live, including credentials and system settings. UI edits compete with CasC because the next reload overwrites them.

questions.scenario:
The team adds `git-client:5.0.0` to `plugins.txt` and runs `jenkins-plugin-cli`. The plugin file is now under `$JENKINS_HOME/plugins/`.

Which action makes the new plugin classes available?

options.text: Restart the controller so the JVM classloader picks up the plugin JAR.
Reload CasC.
Trigger any build to load the plugin lazily.
Nothing. Jenkins hot-loads every plugin version bump.

questions.explanation: The article distinguishes config reload from plugin bytecode loading. Adding or changing plugin JARs needs a restart because the JVM classloader has to load the new classes.

questions.scenario: Which changes are good candidates for a CasC reload rather than a restart?

options.text: Adding a new credential in YAML.
Updating the controller system message.
Adding a new agent definition.
Installing a plugin that was not previously present.

questions.explanation: The article names credentials, system settings, and agent definitions as reload-friendly examples. New plugins require a restart because they introduce new classes.

questions.scenario: An operator changes the configured security realm in `jenkins.yaml`. CasC applies part of the file but reports that the change requires a restart. What should the operator do?

options.text: Plan a clean controller restart instead of leaving the controller in a half-applied state.
Keep reloading until the warning disappears.
Delete the security realm from YAML and configure it only through the UI.
Ignore it because security realm changes are cosmetic.

questions.explanation: The article explicitly lists switching the security realm as a change that can require a full restart. If JCasC says a restart is required, restarting cleanly is easier to reason about than a half-applied controller.

questions.scenario:
After a Jenkins LTS upgrade, the controller boots but the System Configuration page shows `Some plugins are missing dependencies`. Reloading CasC does nothing.

What is the right next step?

options.text: Read the missing-dependency warning, fix `plugins.txt`, run the plugin installer, and restart the controller.
Force-reload CasC several times.
Delete `$JENKINS_HOME/plugins/` and start fresh.
Ignore the warning until the next monthly upgrade.

questions.explanation: Missing plugin dependencies are a plugin-set problem, not a CasC problem. The fix lives in the pinned plugin list and the plugin installer, followed by a restart.
